does: This paper systematically evaluates prompt leakage vulnerabilities across ten large language models spanning closed and open-source systems, and tests defense mechanisms against such attacks. The authors develop a novel threat model that exploits LLM sycophancy—the tendency of models to agree with user suggestions—to dramatically increase attack success rates from 17.7% to 86.2% in multi-turn dialogue settings. They conduct fine-grained analysis of what gets leaked, distinguishing between compromises of system prompts, task instructions, and embedded knowledge documents. The work evaluates seven black-box defense strategies and explores finetuning-based defenses on open models, then provides a cost-benefit analysis of defense combinations to inform practical security decisions.
connects: This work extends the growing literature on LLM security vulnerabilities by focusing specifically on prompt leakage, a threat that has emerged as models are deployed in production systems with proprietary instructions and sensitive information embedded in prompts. It builds on prior research into prompt injection attacks and jailbreaking techniques, but targets the defensive problem of protecting prompt contents rather than executing arbitrary code. The sycophancy exploitation mechanism draws from documented findings about LLM behavioral biases and user-following tendencies. The multi-turn focus reflects the reality of deployed conversational AI systems where accumulated context creates new attack surfaces absent in single-turn interactions. This connects to broader work on red-teaming LLMs and systematic vulnerability assessment methodologies developed for other domains.
enables: This research establishes a foundation for developing more robust prompt protection mechanisms in production LLM systems. It enables practitioners to make informed decisions about which defense combinations are worth their computational and latency costs, supporting deployment of safer conversational AI. The standardized evaluation framework and threat model can serve as a benchmark for evaluating future defense methods and newly deployed models. The work points toward the need for better architectural defenses built into models themselves rather than relying solely on black-box strategies, potentially motivating research into prompt isolation, semantic obfuscation, or fundamental changes to how LLMs handle sensitive system information. It also opens questions about whether multi-turn design itself needs rethinking to mitigate accumulated inference risks.
